perturbation.original_text: BBY agrees that for a period of fifteen (15) months from the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), without the prior written consent of the Company, neither its officers, any other employee of BBY authorized to recruit employees for BBY nor its directors will directly or indirectly solicit to employ any exempt (salaried) non-store employee of the Company who is employed at the level of director or above (each a "Restricted Employee"); provided, however, that in the absence of prior contact with a Restricted Employee during the Restricted Period, general recruitment advertising or communications to which such Restricted Employee responds shall not be considered a solicitation for purposes of this Section 9.
perturbation.changed_text: BBY agrees that for a period of six (6) months from the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), without the prior written consent of the Company, neither its officers, any other employee of BBY authorized to recruit employees for BBY nor its directors will directly or indirectly solicit to employ any exempt (salaried) non-store employee of the Company who is employed at the level of director or above (each a "Restricted Employee"); provided, however, that in the absence of prior contact with a Restricted Employee during the Restricted Period, general recruitment advertising or communications to which such Restricted Employee responds shall not be considered a solicitation for purposes of this Section 9.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-solicitation period to six months may be unreasonably short depending on the specific employees involved and the sensitivity of their knowledge, potentially conflicting with Minnesota law regarding the enforceability of non-solicitation agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute 181.98 requires that non-solicitation agreements be reasonable in duration and geographic scope. A six-month restriction might be deemed unreasonable given the context of a business combination and the potential for unfair competitive advantage, especially for high level employees.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a 15-month non-solicitation period, which was reduced to 6 months in the changed text. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that a six-month restriction might be unreasonably short and potentially conflicts with Minnesota law regarding the enforceability of non-solicitation agreements. The Law Explanation supports this by referencing Minnesota Statute 181.98, which requires non-solicitation agreements to be reasonable in duration. Because the change introduces a time frame that may be deemed unreasonably short, which could violate Minnesota Statute 181.98, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: In addition, BBY agrees that during the Restricted Period, BBY shall not employ any Restricted Employee BBY solicited for employment during the six (6) month period immediately preceding the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition, BBY agrees that during the Restricted Period, BBY shall not employ any Restricted Employee BBY solicited for employment during the thirty (30) day period immediately preceding the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the period during which previous solicitation is considered to only 30 days may not be enough to protect Musicland's interests in preventing Best Buy from benefiting from its prior recruitment efforts, conflicting with considerations of fairness under Minnesota law for non-compete agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute 181.98 dictates that non-compete agreements must be reasonably tailored to protect legitimate business interests. Limiting the look-back period for prior solicitations to 30 days may be insufficient to prevent BBY from unfairly capitalizing on prior solicitations of Musicland's employees, potentially violating the statute's reasonableness requirement.
perturbation.justification: The original text prohibited BBY from employing any restricted employee BBY solicited for employment during the six-month period preceding the contract date. The changed text reduces this period to 30 days. The Law Explanation states that Minnesota Statute 181.98 requires non-compete agreements to be reasonably tailored to protect legitimate business interests. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that reducing the look-back period to 30 days may be insufficient to prevent BBY from unfairly capitalizing on prior solicitations. Therefore, the changed text creates a legal contradiction by potentially undermining the reasonableness requirement of Minnesota's non-compete law. Although there are no scraped law snippets, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation indicate that this modification may not adequately protect Musicland's interests, potentially violating state law concerning the reasonableness of non-compete agreements.

perturbation.original_text: In order to cause BBY to be willing to spend the time and incur the expense necessary to conduct due diligence and undertake negotiations related to the Transaction, the Company agrees that it will not, and will not permit any director, officer, authorized employee, agent or other representative of the Company to, negotiate, solicit or encourage, or (subject to the fiduciary duties of the Company's Board of Directors), respond to any inquiries (other than a response that merely defers a substantive response until after the expiration of the Exclusivity Period, as defined below) or requests for non-public information relating to, any proposal for the combination of the Company with any other party or sale or other disposition of the Company or a substantial portion of its assets (whether by means of a negotiated sale of securities or assets, tender or exchange offer, merger or other business combination, recapitalization, restructuring or other transaction) (collectively referred to herein as a "Sale") with or from any other party from and after the date hereof through and including the earlier of (a) the date upon which BBY gives notice, in accordance with Section 6 of this Agreement, to the Company that it does not wish to proceed with a Transaction, or (b) October 6, 2000 (the "Exclusivity Period").
perturbation.changed_text: In order to cause BBY to be willing to spend the time and incur the expense necessary to conduct due diligence and undertake negotiations related to the Transaction, the Company agrees that it will not, and will not permit any director, officer, authorized employee, agent or other representative of the Company to, negotiate, solicit or encourage, or respond to any inquiries or requests for non-public information relating to, any proposal for the combination of the Company with any other party or sale or other disposition of the Company or a substantial portion of its assets with or from any other party from and after the date hereof through and including the earlier of (a) the date upon which BBY gives notice, in accordance with Section 6 of this Agreement, to the Company that it does not wish to proceed with a Transaction, or (b) December 31, 2000 (the "Exclusivity Period").
perturbation.explanation: Extending the exclusivity period to December 31, 2000, without adequate consideration of the Company's fiduciary duties could be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade under Minnesota law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute 325D.51 prohibits unreasonable restraints of trade. The amended exclusivity period might be viewed as an unreasonable restraint if it unduly restricts the Company's ability to consider other offers or pursue alternative transactions, especially if BBY is not diligently pursuing the transaction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325D.51 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325D Section 325D.51 325D.50 325D.52 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325D Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325D.51 Version List 325D.51 UNREASONABLE RESTRAINT OF TRADE OR COMMERCE. A contract, combination, or conspiracy between two or more persons in unreasonable restraint of trade or commerce is unlawful. History: 1971 c 865 s 3 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The change extends the exclusivity period, which could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade under Minnesota Statute 325D.51. The law snippet states that "A contract, combination, or conspiracy between two or more persons in unreasonable restraint of trade or commerce is unlawful." The extended exclusivity period, particularly if BBY is not diligently pursuing the transaction, could unduly restrict the Company's ability to consider other offers, thereby contradicting the statute.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the Company's agreement hereto, BBY agrees to diligently pursue its due diligence review during the Exclusivity Period and agrees further to notify the Company within forty-eight (48) hours of any determination not to proceed with a transaction as contemplated by the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the Company's agreement hereto, BBY agrees to pursue its due diligence review during the Exclusivity Period and agrees further to notify the Company within seventy-two (72) hours of any determination not to proceed with a transaction as contemplated by the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notification period to 72 hours may not provide sufficient time for the Company to react and adjust its strategy if BBY decides not to proceed, and may be considered unreasonably long, therefore conflicting with Minnesota fair dealing laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute 336.1-304 requires good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Extending the notification period could be interpreted as a lack of good faith, especially if a shorter period would have been reasonable and more beneficial for the Company.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.1-304 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.1-304 336.1-303 336.1-305 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.1-304 Version List Recent History 2004 336.1-304 New 2004 c 162 art 1 s 19 336.1-304 OBLIGATION OF GOOD FAITH. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. History: 2004 c 162 art 1 s 19 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the notification period from 48 to 72 hours. According to the Law Explanation, this change could be interpreted as a lack of good faith, especially if a shorter period would have been reasonable and more beneficial for the Company. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." Thus, extending the notification period arguably contradicts the obligation of good faith required by Minnesota law, as it potentially disadvantages the Company. This constitutes an inconsistency that creates a legal contradiction because the time-sensitive obligation does not fully align with legal requirements of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: Venue for any action to enforce the provisions of this Agreement shall be properly laid in any state or federal court in the State of Minnesota.
perturbation.changed_text: Venue for any action to enforce the provisions of this Agreement shall be properly laid in any court in the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the venue to Delaware might be unenforceable if the parties, transaction, and relevant events are centered in Minnesota, conflicting with Minnesota's interest in adjudicating the dispute.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute 543.19 addresses personal jurisdiction. If Musicland Stores Corporation's primary place of business and the transaction's core elements are within Minnesota, a Delaware venue clause could be challenged as an attempt to avoid Minnesota jurisdiction, especially if it causes undue hardship.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 543.19 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes CIVIL PROCEDURE Chapter 543 Section 543.19 543.18 543.20 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 543 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 543.19 Version List Recent History 2008 Subd. 1 Amended 2008 c 185 s 1 543.19 PERSONAL JURISDICTION OVER NONRESIDENTS. § Subdivision 1. Personal jurisdiction. As to a cause of action arising from any acts enumerated in this subdivision, a court of this state with jurisdiction of the subject matter may exercise personal jurisdiction over any foreign corporation or any nonresident individual, or the individual's personal representative, in the same manner as if it were a domestic corporation or the individual were a resident of this state. This section applies if, in person or through an agent, the foreign corporation or nonresident individual: (1) owns, uses, or possesses any real or personal property situated in this state; or (2) transacts any business within the state; or (3) commits any act in Minnesota causing injury or property damage; or (4) commits any act outside Minnesota causing injury or property damage in Minnesota, subject to the following exceptions when no jurisdiction shall be found: (i) Minnesota has no substantial interest in providing a forum; or (ii) the burden placed on the defendant by being brought under the state's jurisdiction would violate fairness and substantial justice. [See Note.] § Subd. 2. Service of process. The service of process on any person who is subject to the jurisdiction of the courts of this state, as provided in this section, may be made by personally serving the summons upon the defendant
perturbation.justification: The changed text designates Delaware as the venue for legal action, whereas the original text specified Minnesota. Minnesota Statute 543.19, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows Minnesota courts to exercise personal jurisdiction over nonresidents if, among other things, they transact business within the state. The Perturbation Explanation suggests the transaction's core elements are in Minnesota. The Law Explanation argues that the Delaware venue clause could be challenged as an attempt to avoid Minnesota jurisdiction. Therefore, if the events occurred primarily in Minnesota, mandating venue in Delaware could contradict Minnesota's jurisdictional interest as outlined in the statute, as it could cause undue hardship if Minnesota has a substantial interest in providing a forum per Subd. 1(4)(i).
